turns.text: Hello Alvera, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s… honestly, it’s a lot. I appreciate you wanting to listen, but it’s hard to put into words when I’m still just trying to survive the next hour. Most days, the drive from Sandisfield into the city feels like an eternity. By the time I get to the hospital for these check-ups, my ankles are so swollen I can hardly stand, and that’s before I even climb onto that crinkly exam table.

People keep telling me, "You’ve done this three times before, you’re a pro." But that’s the thing—I’m not. This is twins. And this "pre-eclampsia" label... it just feels like someone put a countdown clock on my chest. My doctor, she’s perfectly capable, I know that. She’s efficient. But she talks about "delivery management" and "protocols" like I’m a malfunctioning piece of machinery. I look at her and I just want to scream, "Don't you see me?" 

I’m terrified. I’m scared that my body is going to let these babies down right when we’re inches from the finish line. Every time my head throbs or I feel that panic rising, I feel like I'm losing my grip. I just want to be a mother again, not a "high-risk complication." I just want to bring them home.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

You know, before I got that "high-risk" label slapped onto my chart, I had this neat, typed-out document. I spent hours reading about birth plans online, thinking I could organize the experience—some music, maybe dimmed lights, a sense of autonomy. I really thought that if I articulated what I wanted, the medical staff would see me as a partner in the process.

But once the pre-eclampsia became the focus, that plan felt like a scrap of paper in a hurricane. When I brought it up, I could see the doctor’s eyes glaze over. It was like, "That’s cute, Alvera, but let’s talk about the blood pressure cuffs and the constant monitoring." They didn't see a plan; they saw a nuisance that might interfere with their protocols. 

When things started, it wasn’t a "birth experience" anymore—it was a medical event. There was no room for my preferences because the room was filled with people checking monitors, adjusting fluids, and whispering about spikes in my pressure. I wasn't consulted; I was managed. I felt like I had completely lost control. All that work I did, all the hope I put into having a say, it just evaporated. It made me realize that when you're "high-risk," your voice is the first thing that gets sidelined.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

It wasn't one single moment; it was more like a slow, crushing realization. I had wanted, more than anything, to stay mobile as long as I could. I know my body—I know what labor feels like—and I needed to move, to sway, to find my own rhythm to keep the panic at bay. 

But as soon as I was in the room, the monitors went on. I was tethered to the bed by cords and IV lines because of the pre-eclampsia. When I asked if I could stand, the nurse’s look was so pitying, almost apologetic, but firm: "Alvera, we need that constant trace to watch the babies' heart rates." It felt like being anchored to a sinking ship. Every time I tried to shift or find a comfortable position, a sensor would slip, an alarm would chirp, and someone would rush in to readjust me.

It was invasive and exhausting. I wasn't just losing the mobility I’d hoped for; I was losing my connection to my own body. I felt like a patient, not a mother-to-be. Watching my husband stand there, helpless, while the nurses focused entirely on the screens instead of me... it was isolating. My "plan" didn't matter because the monitors were the only thing they were really listening to. I felt so small, just a vessel for these two babies, while my own fear was completely pushed into the corner.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I think I went in with this naive idea that pain management would be a conversation—that we’d talk about the trade-offs, how I was feeling, and what I could handle. With my first three, I felt like I had a say, like I was navigating the discomfort on my own terms. 

But with the twins, the conversation never really happened. It felt like the options were decided for me before I even walked through the door. Because of the pre-eclampsia and the constant, suffocating fear that my blood pressure was going to spike if I got too stressed, the focus was entirely on "keeping me calm." To them, that meant an epidural was the only logical choice. It wasn't presented as a preference; it was presented as the safe, responsible thing to do. When I hesitated, or asked if I could try breathing or changing positions first, the staff looked at me like I was being reckless. 

The reality was that my pain wasn't managed; it was silenced. The epidural took away the intensity, yes, but it also took away my agency. I felt so disconnected from the process, just waiting for the machines to tell me when it was time to push. I wanted support, not just a procedure, but it felt like they were managing a medical emergency rather than supporting a woman giving birth.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I really wanted to try water immersion. I had heard so much about how the warmth and the buoyancy can take the edge off the intensity, especially with the weight of twins pulling on my back. I thought if I could just get into a tub, I could find that headspace where the fear doesn't paralyze me. I wanted to feel like a person again, not a medical puzzle.

But when I brought it up, the answer was a flat "no." It was all about the "high-risk" status again. Because I needed continuous electronic fetal monitoring to watch for signs of distress—and because they were so worried about my blood pressure—they said it just wasn't "compatible" with their safety protocols. It wasn’t a conversation about how we might make it work; it was just a wall. 

It’s hard to describe how that feels. It’s not just about the pain; it’s about having your autonomy chipped away, piece by piece. Everything I wanted for comfort was dismissed as a risk to the outcome they were chasing. I felt like the staff was so focused on keeping the clinical numbers in the "safe" zone that they completely ignored the fact that I was actually there, experiencing it. I wasn't allowed to have a softer, more human birth because I was categorized as a liability. It left me feeling incredibly lonely in that room.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I think I pictured the hospital as a place where, even if things were high-risk, I’d be treated like a person first and a patient second. I wanted to feel like I was arriving at a place of care, where someone would look at me—the person, the mother—and say, "We’re going to help you through this together." I wanted it to feel, if not warm, then at least grounded. 

The reality was clinical, cold, and jarring. As soon as I walked through those double doors, the atmosphere felt sterile and frantic. It wasn’t a place of "welcome"; it was a place of processing. I felt like I was being moved along an assembly line. The lights were too bright, the air conditioning was freezing, and the constant hum of machines just amplified that fluttering panic in my chest. 

There was no pause to ask how I was holding up mentally, or to acknowledge that I was scared. It was all about the paperwork, the blood pressure cuff, and the triage tags. I felt like an intruder in a space that belonged entirely to the medical staff. It was incredibly isolating to walk into that room feeling like I had just handed over my body and my autonomy to a team that saw me as a series of complications to be managed rather than a woman about to bring life into the world.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most jarring part—was just how much the room felt like a cockpit instead of a place where a person was supposed to bring two babies into the world. I expected hospital rooms to be medical, sure, but I didn't expect it to feel so… hostile to anything human. 

When they led me to the labor and delivery room, the first thing that hit me wasn't the warmth or the help; it was the noise. There were monitors chirping and beeping from every corner, and the light felt surgical, like it was designed to show every flaw rather than provide comfort. I remember standing there in my gown, shivering, looking at all these screens and cords, and thinking, *Where do I go in here?* There wasn’t a single space for me. Even the chair for my husband looked uncomfortable, like it was an afterthought. 

I was shocked by how quickly they stripped away my sense of privacy. Within minutes, there were people coming in and out, not really making eye contact, just looking at the screens. It was like I had walked onto a movie set for a medical drama where I was just the prop in the middle. The atmosphere was so thick with "risk" that it felt like there wasn't enough oxygen in the room for me to actually breathe.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I expected—or maybe I just desperately hoped—that if things took a turn, the doctors would stop, look me in the eye, and treat me like a partner. I thought they would explain what was happening, give me the pros and cons, and let me be part of the decision. I didn't expect to be in total control, but I did expect to be a participant.

The reality was worlds apart. When the twins’ heart rates dipped, the room didn't become a place of collaboration; it became a place of frantic, efficient, silent commands. It was like I suddenly became invisible. The doctor and nurses started barking at each other, not at me. They’d say things like "I'm starting an infusion" or "we need to prep for an internal," and it was all directed over my head, as if I weren't even there.

When I tried to ask, "What’s happening? Is everyone okay?" the responses were clipped, rushed, and dismissive. They didn’t explain; they just *did*. They’d physically move my legs or shove a mask onto my face without waiting for me to process or agree. It felt like I was being handled, not helped. I wasn’t informed; I was managed. That lack of communication was more terrifying than the medical complications themselves—it turned a scary situation into a nightmare where I had zero agency.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

I didn't have choices. Not in the moments that mattered. When the room fills with people moving in a blur, and the beeping of the machines gets faster, "choice" feels like a cruel joke. 

Decisions didn’t happen *with* me; they happened *to* me. I remember someone saying something like, "We’re going to need to break your water," or "We’re increasing the pitocin," and it was phrased as a statement, not a question. It was delivered with this tone of "We are the experts, this is what must happen, just stay still." If I tried to hesitate, there was this heavy, impatient silence that made it clear that to question them was to endanger my babies. And what mother is going to choose the "riskier" option when they’ve already made you feel like you’re already failing?

It’s the most helpless I’ve ever felt. It’s like being a passenger in a car where the driver is going way too fast, refusing to let you touch the wheel, and acting like they’re doing you a favor by keeping you alive. I wasn't an active participant in my own birth. I was just the location where the medical intervention was taking place. That feeling of being a bystander to my own body... it still haunts me.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I think I walked into that hospital hoping for someone who would be my anchor. With all the chaos of the twins and the pre-eclampsia, I needed someone to look at me and acknowledge that this was hard—to hold my hand and tell me I was doing a good job, not just checking the blood pressure cuff. I expected, or at least hoped for, a bit of warmth. Someone to act like a person rather than just a technician.

But the reality? It was almost exclusively transactional. The staff were professional—I’ll give them that—but it was a cold, efficient kind of professionalism. They were there to manage the clinical data, not to support the human behind the numbers. If I was crying or struggling to catch my breath, they didn’t stop to comfort me. They just adjusted the monitors or checked my charts. It felt like my emotional state was treated as just another symptom to be managed—if I was anxious, they’d focus on the medication or the monitoring to "fix" it, rather than just sitting with me or acknowledging that I was terrified. 

I felt so incredibly lonely, even with people filling the room. My husband tried to be my rock, but even he felt intimidated by them. We were just two people in the middle of a swarm of experts who were looking everywhere *except* at us.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I remember a specific moment when I was struggling—the contractions were coming in waves, and the monitors were screaming because the twins’ heart rates were dipping again. I was terrified, just paralyzed with the thought that my body was failing them, and I reached out, hoping for a touch or a reassuring word from the nurse. 

Instead, she didn't even look at my face. She just reached over me, checked the monitor settings, and muttered something to the resident about "maternal baseline shifts." I wasn't Alvera in that moment; I was just the bed, just the equipment. I felt so small and so deeply, profoundly alone. Even though my husband was right there holding my hand, the medical staff had effectively sucked all the air out of the room. It was like I had become a ghost in my own body.

There was never a moment where someone sat down, looked me in the eyes, and said, "I know you're scared, and I'm right here with you." It was just a constant stream of clinical assessments. That isolation—that feeling of being a "high-risk complication" instead of a mother—is what sticks with me more than the pain itself. You can handle the physical hurt, but you can’t handle the feeling that nobody in the room is actually looking at you.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this picture in my head—a quiet, soft moment. I expected that as soon as the first baby was out, they’d be placed right on my chest. I wanted to feel their skin against mine, to finally hear them cry and know that the "ticking clock" had stopped, that we had both made it to the other side. I pictured the room settling down, the lights dimming, just me, my husband, and our babies.

But it was nothing like that. The reality was a blur of bright lights and sharp movements. Because they were twins and there had been so much talk of "high risk" all along, it felt like the birth didn't end when they were delivered; it just turned into a different kind of clinical emergency. They were whisked away to a warming table almost immediately. There was so much activity—suctioning, checking, weighing, people shouting out numbers—that I felt like a spectator to my own children’s first breaths. 

I couldn’t hold them. I couldn’t even see their faces properly. I just lay there on that table, exhausted and shivering, watching them be poked and prodded from a distance. It was the most surreal, heartbreaking disappointment. I had spent months terrified of losing them, and in those first precious minutes, I still felt like they didn't really belong to me yet—they belonged to the team of people surrounding them. I just wanted to reach out, but I felt too drained and too shut out to even ask.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I honestly thought the "hard part" would end the second the babies were out. I expected that once the birth was done, the pre-eclampsia, the swelling, and that constant, suffocating fear would just… vanish. I thought my body would start to feel like *mine* again, that I’d be able to walk without pain, hold my babies, and start the slow, steady process of being a mother of twins. I figured it would be exhausting, of course—who sleeps with twins?—but I expected it to be a physical healing I could understand.

The reality was so much heavier. That weight in my chest, the "ticking clock" feeling? It didn't just disappear. Even after we were home, I kept waiting for the other shoe to drop. My blood pressure remained a ghost I had to keep chasing, and the physical recovery was brutal because my mind wouldn’t let my body rest. Every time I had a headache, I spiraled, wondering if it was a complication returning. 

I didn't expect the emotional recovery to be harder than the C-section incision. I felt so hollowed out, like the hospital had taken not just my babies, but my sense of safety and my own strength. I spent those first weeks sitting in the nursery, terrified of every sound, feeling like I was still just a medical case waiting for a bad result, even when the babies were finally right there in my arms. I wasn't just recovering from surgery; I was recovering from feeling invisible for so long.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think I had these romanticized, almost effortless visions of it. I assumed, because it’s "natural," that my body would just know what to do and the babies would just latch, and we’d settle into this peaceful rhythm. I expected the nurses in the hospital to be like guides—gentle, encouraging, helping me find the right position, especially with two of them. I wanted that connection, that moment where I finally felt like I was doing something *right* after feeling like I’d done everything wrong medically.

The reality was… chaotic. It was like everything else in that hospital: rushed and clinical. My body was still recovering from the surgery and the shock of the whole ordeal, and my milk didn’t just magically appear. I was sitting there, trying to manage two tiny, crying, hungry infants, and every time I asked for help, it felt like the staff was watching the clock. They’d come in, shove a pillow under my arm, point at the latch, and then rush out to the next room. There was no patience, no warmth, no acknowledging that I was completely overwhelmed. 

At home, it became this relentless cycle of tracking ounces and timing sessions. It wasn’t about bonding anymore; it felt like a job, like I was still being monitored by my own anxiety. I’d be crying while feeding one, worrying if the other one was okay, or if my supply was enough. It felt like I was fighting my own body again. I just wanted to feel capable, but instead, I felt like a failure because nothing was as easy as the books promised. It wasn't the sweet, quiet bonding time I had dreamt of; it was just one more thing I felt like I was barely keeping afloat.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

There was a massive gap. The support I was offered was purely functional. It was like they were teaching me how to operate a machine—"Put the baby here, aim the mouth there, keep track of the time." They treated it like a mechanical task, and if the machine didn't latch perfectly, they’d just hand me a bottle or a nipple shield, check a box on their chart, and move on. They didn't see the woman who was exhausted, traumatized, and sobbing because she felt so utterly detached from her own babies.

What I actually needed was someone to acknowledge the weight of it all. I needed someone to sit with me, maybe for twenty minutes, and just tell me it was okay to be struggling, that it was okay to feel like I was failing, and to help me feel confident as a mother rather than a patient. I didn't need a tutorial on breastfeeding mechanics; I needed someone to help me connect with them, to help me feel like *I* was the one feeding them, not the hospital. 

Instead, I felt rushed, judged, and alone. When the help is just a list of instructions delivered by someone who can’t wait to get out of the room, you don't feel supported—you feel like a problem they’re trying to solve so they can move on to the next one.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I expected the "fog" of pregnancy to just lift. I’d spent months with that heavy, cold weight of pre-eclampsia, and I truly believed that once I was holding my babies, the light would come back. I imagined I’d feel tired, sure—who wouldn't with twins?—but I thought I’d feel like a mother. I expected to finally breathe, like I’d promised myself, and to just settle into this new, beautiful rhythm.

The reality was that the fear didn't leave when the babies arrived; it just changed shape. I felt like I was wearing a mask of "fine" for everyone else, while inside, I was still waiting for something to go wrong. I’d be nursing them, and suddenly my heart would race, and I’d be checking my blood pressure monitor again, convinced that the dizziness was the pre-eclampsia coming back for me. 

I felt this strange, deep detachment, too. It’s hard to bond when you still feel like a patient who hasn't been discharged from the trauma of the birth. I spent so many nights awake, staring at them, not feeling joy, but feeling this overwhelming, crushing responsibility and a haunting loneliness. I felt like I had been stripped of my own agency, and I didn’t know how to reclaim the woman I was before the "high-risk" label took over my life. It was quieter than the hospital, but in some ways, the silence made the anxiety even louder. I wasn't just recovering; I was trying to figure out who I was outside of a medical chart.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

There was a massive gap. It felt like there was this script I had to follow: "You have two healthy babies, you’re home, you survived the high-risk birth—you should be overjoyed." That’s what society, my family, and even the doctors expected of me. Anything other than pure, glowing bliss felt like a betrayal of the fact that we had made it through.

Whenever I tried to voice that I was drowning—that the anxiety was still there, that I felt detached, or that the trauma of that hospital room was still rattling around in my head—I’d get these sympathetic, glassy-eyed nods. "You're a warrior," they’d say. "You did it." It felt dismissive. Being called a "warrior" felt like a way to shut me up. It was like they were saying, *You’re fixed now, the babies are fine, stop bringing up the scary stuff.*

I wasn't *allowed* to be traumatized because I had been "successful." I felt like I had to hide the panic because if I let it show, people would think I was failing as a mother. I was so angry at myself, too. I felt guilty for not being instantly, perfectly happy. I was mourning the birth experience I lost, the control I never had, and the person I used to be before I became a "complication." But how do you tell people you’re grieving when you’re supposed to be celebrating? You just swallow it and keep moving.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before this, I imagined that becoming a mother—a mother to *twins*, no less—would be this seamless expansion of who I was. I thought I’d just settle into this rhythm, like a missing puzzle piece had finally clicked into place. I expected to feel more grounded, more "me," just with a little more exhaustion and a lot more love.

The reality? I feel like I was hollowed out and then rebuilt by people who didn't know the first thing about me. The "high-risk" label didn't just label my medical status; it seemed to overwrite my personality. When I look in the mirror now, I don't just see a mother. I see a survivor of a system that didn't think I mattered as a person. I feel like I lost the ability to trust my own body—that body that I once felt so in tune with before the monitors, the needles, and the constant, cold efficiency of the hospital.

My identity feels fractured. There’s the woman who loves her children more than life itself, and then there’s this other version of me—the one who is constantly scanning for the next emergency, the one who is still waiting for someone to give her permission to breathe. I used to be Alvera, a person with her own rhythm. Now, I feel like I’m a vessel for these two beautiful babies and a collection of medical checklists. I’m still trying to find the woman I was before the fear took over, but some days, it feels like she’s a stranger I’m trying to remember.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

It’s like all the pieces of who I was before—the woman who worked, who liked quiet, who felt in control of her own body—were systematically erased the moment I stepped into that hospital. I thought I’d be able to toggle back into those roles, even if just in small ways, but the "high-risk" label acts like a gravitational pull. It demands 100% of your energy, and suddenly, my old identity felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.

The hardest part to hold onto was my sense of being a competent adult. At work, I was someone who handled things, someone who made decisions. In that hospital, I was a patient, a complication, a body on a bed. It’s hard to reconcile those two versions of myself. I find myself doubting my own judgment now, even in simple things, because the medical environment made me feel like I didn’t know how to keep myself or my babies safe without their intervention.

Socially, I feel like I’m wearing a suit of armor. I want to talk to my friends about how I’m *really* doing, but they look at me like I’m a "success story" of modern medicine. They don’t want to hear about the haunting, the panic, or the loss of agency. Holding onto "me" means holding onto a version of my history that the rest of the world wants to write over with a happy ending. It's exhausting to be the only person who remembers the trauma.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected the follow-up care to be a bridge back to "normal." I thought, *Okay, the birth was a crisis, but now we’re in the recovery phase, and these appointments will be where I finally get to be a person again.* I expected someone to check on *me*—my stitches, my hormones, but mostly my head. I thought they would ask, "Alvera, how are you handling the memory of that room?" or "Do you feel like you’re back in your own skin?"

The reality was a hollow, clinical checklist. The check-ups were short, focused entirely on the babies’ growth charts and my physical healing—blood pressure, incision check, "Everything looks good, you’re cleared for exercise." It was like they were checking the box for "postnatal physical clearance" and considering the file closed.

When I tried to mention the panic, the flashbacks, or that constant, gnawing feeling of being invisible, it was brushed off as "normal postpartum adjustment" or "just the twins' exhaustion." They were so efficient, so focused on the data points—weight, pressure, development—that there was zero space to talk about the trauma. I’d leave those appointments feeling more isolated than when I walked in. They saw a "healthy" mother and a "healthy" set of twins, and that was all the evidence they needed to move on. They couldn't see that inside, I was still stuck in that hospital room, waiting for someone to finally notice *me*.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

It was less about the doctors saying "no" and more about how they just kind of… pivoted away. I remember telling my GP, about six weeks in, that I felt like I was still living in that hospital room. I told her I had these moments where my heart would just hammer in my chest for no reason, and that I felt like I couldn’t trust my own body anymore because I’d spent so long being told what it was "doing" by monitors and charts.

She looked at me, gave me that tight, clinical smile, and said something like, "That's completely expected with twins, Alvera. You're exhausted. It's a huge adjustment. Make sure you're getting some sleep when you can." 

Sleep? With twins? It felt so dismissive. She didn't ask *what* the fear felt like, or if I wanted to talk to someone about the birth itself. She checked my blood pressure—which was fine, so in her mind, the "problem" was solved—and moved on to checking the babies’ development. It was like my mental health was just an annoying side note to the real work of tracking their growth. 

I left feeling smaller than when I arrived. It’s like they were only equipped to handle the physical machine, and if the "machine" was functioning, they assumed the person inside was fine. I didn't raise it again. What was the point? They’d already decided I was a success story.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I thought returning to work would be my anchor, my way back to being "Alvera" again. I missed the structure, the feeling of having a brain that worked on things other than feedings and heart-rate monitoring. I expected to feel professional, competent—like the person who existed before the "high-risk" label took over my life. I pictured myself sitting at my desk, drinking my coffee, finally feeling like I wasn’t just a patient anymore.

The reality was a mess of sensory overload and constant, low-level panic. My brain felt like it had been rewired in that hospital room, and it wouldn't switch back. I’d be in a meeting, staring at a spreadsheet, and suddenly a wave of anxiety would hit me—not about the work, but about the babies. I’d check my phone constantly, waiting for a call from daycare, convinced that something was going to go wrong, just like it did during the birth.

It was exhausting to perform "competent employee" while feeling like I was vibrating with anxiety. My coworkers see a mother who’s "bouncing back," but they don’t see the way I hold my breath when my phone buzzes. I feel disconnected from my job, too. I’m doing the tasks, but a part of me is still back in that sterile, cold delivery room, waiting for someone to ask me how I am. It’s like I’m living two lives, and neither one of them feels like home.
Were there practical aspects of returning to work — feeding, childcare, your own health — that were harder to manage than you expected?

Everything was harder, mostly because I felt like I was physically split in two. Managing the feeding schedule with twins was the biggest hurdle—I’d be in a conference room, counting down the minutes until I had to pump, feeling like my body was doing this weird, biological tug-of-war while I was supposed to be talking about spreadsheets or deadlines. It felt incredibly invasive and bizarre to have to excuse myself and hook up a machine in a closet, and every time the pump hummed, it reminded me of those monitors in the hospital, buzzing and chirping. 

Then there was the constant, nagging doubt about childcare. I couldn't just "leave them." I kept calling, just to hear them breathe on the other end, convinced that if I wasn't watching them, something would go wrong. I felt like I had to prove I was just as "on top of it" as I was before, but my brain was just… thin. I was so stretched. 

My own physical recovery was still trailing behind me like a heavy weight, too. I’d get these dizzy spells or sudden headaches, and I’d freeze, terrified it was the pre-eclampsia, even though logically I knew it was probably just exhaustion. I wasn't just working; I was managing a constant, internal emergency while trying to act like everything was perfectly normal. It was exhausting.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I pictured us as a team. Before the birth, we talked about "tag-teaming" the twins, about how we’d lean on each other to get through the sleep deprivation and the shift in our lives. I imagined us sitting together at the end of the day, laughing over the chaos, feeling closer because we were building a family together. I really thought the experience would solidify that "us against the world" feeling.

The reality has been much lonelier. My husband, he’s been a rock, but he saw the birth differently. He wasn't the one feeling the doctors’ needles or the constant, invasive monitoring. He wasn't the one whose body was the "problem." He saw us survive, and he saw two healthy babies, so in his mind, the crisis was over. When I try to explain that I’m still feeling haunted by that hospital room, or that I feel disconnected because of how I was treated, he gets quiet. He wants to move forward, and he wants me to move forward with him.

It’s created this silent distance between us. I feel like he’s living in a world where everything is "fine" because the babies are here, and I’m still living in the world where I feel like I lost a part of myself. I want to share the fear, but I’m terrified that if I let him see how deep it goes, he’ll just see me as broken. So, I hold it in. We’re co-parenting—we’re efficient, we’re functional—but we aren't necessarily *sharing* the emotional weight. It feels like we’re two people standing in the same room, looking at different things.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the most painful one—was discovering that we went through the same event but walked away with entirely different realities. I thought going through something as "high-risk" as this birth would weld us together, like a shared battle. But the reality is that the birth experience was so intensely individual. 

I was the one whose body was the vessel, the one being monitored, the one who lost control and felt completely objectified. To him, the outcome was the priority: the babies arrived, they were healthy, and that was the victory. He looks at them and sees our future; I look at them and sometimes I see the trauma of the room where they were born. 

When I’m struggling or having a moment where I feel that familiar, sharp anxiety, he tries to be the "fixer"—he tells me to rest, or he tries to distract me with a joke. He’s trying to be a good partner, but it feels like he’s trying to steer me away from the things I need to process. It surprised me how quickly we started operating like colleagues managing a project rather than lovers navigating a transition. We are a fantastic team when it comes to the logistics of two babies, but there’s this unspoken boundary where I can't let him in on the fear, because I don't want to be the "weak" one who’s still stuck in the hospital. We’re close, but we’re drifting in different currents.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be a bridge. I thought it would be a partnership—a team of experts who would help me navigate the medical unknowns of a high-risk twin pregnancy while respecting me as a person. I expected that even if things got complicated, there would be a sense of humanity, a feeling that I was being held by a system designed to look after both my physical safety and my emotional well-being.

The most significant gap, looking back at the whole arc, is the complete absence of "whole-person" care. I received excellent *technical* care—the clinical stuff, the fetal monitoring, the blood pressure management—but I received almost zero *human* care. I felt like a vessel they were troubleshooting. The system was so hyper-focused on the clinical outcomes that it treated my fear, my autonomy, and my experience as obstacles to be pushed aside. 

They kept my babies safe, and I know I should be grateful, but they did it at the expense of my sense of self. They didn't see Alvera; they saw a set of vitals. I walked out of that hospital with two healthy children, but I left behind my sense of agency and my trust in my own body. The gap wasn't in their skill; it was in their vision. They saved the babies, but they never once truly met the mother.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be to completely dismantle this idea that "high-risk" means the woman’s humanity has to be suspended. The system is designed to prioritize data points over the person behind them, and that focus on "clinical efficiency" is exactly what strips you of your autonomy. I’d want the system to be designed to integrate emotional safety as a primary indicator of "success," right alongside blood pressure and fetal heart rates.

Right now, if your vitals are stable, you’re considered a success. I want a system where a woman being terrified, feeling silenced, or losing her sense of agency is treated as a clinical complication, too. I’d want "birth partners" or advocates—maybe a dedicated trauma-informed liaison—whose job isn't to look at a monitor, but to look at *me*. Someone to translate the medical jargon, to hold space for the fear, and to make sure that I am actually making the decisions about my own body, rather than having them done to me in a rush of noise and alarms.

If you’re "high-risk," you already feel like you’re losing control. The system should be designed to give you that control back, not to take more away. It needs to stop seeing us as patients to be processed and start seeing us as mothers who are trying to survive one of the most vulnerable moments of our lives.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think... I think the hardest part is that I don’t know if I’ll ever fully forgive the system for making me feel so small. When you’re in that hospital, you are in a state of absolute, profound surrender. You’re in a gown, you’re in pain, and you’re terrified for your babies’ lives. You are the very definition of vulnerable. 

I wish someone had told me, right at the start, that my fear wasn’t a failure. I wish they had acknowledged that just because my body was "high-risk," it didn't mean my voice didn't count anymore. People talk about the "miracle of birth" like it’s a fairy tale, but for some of us, it’s a battle, and sometimes you come out of that battle feeling like you’ve been stripped of your own identity. 

I just want whoever reads this to know that even when the outcome is "successful"—even when the babies go home healthy, and the doctors pat themselves on the back—the mother might still be grieving the person she was before the trauma of the care she received. We need to be seen as more than just a successful outcome. I am still here, still trying to put the pieces of myself back together, and I just wish that for once, someone would ask me how *I* am doing, and really, truly listen to the answer. That’s all. Thank you for listening to me today.
